drafts.draft: Most small businesses don't have a marketing problem. They have a follow-up problem. The leads are already there. Nobody chases them on Tuesday at 6pm.
If a customer asks at 9pm and you answer at 9am, the sale is already gone. Speed has quietly become the only differentiator left.
Founders spend a year building the perfect onboarding then lose 40% of signups because nobody replies to 'is this legit?' for six hours.

drafts.target_text: A cybersecurity firm contacted us less than 24 hours before going to the media with vague claims about Replit. This is not how responsible security research works.
drafts.draft: Curious if you'll publish the actual report when it lands. The 24h window is the giveaway: real disclosure people don't do that.

drafts.target_text: You're moving forward. But is it actually progress?
drafts.draft: The trap is mistaking activity for progress. Shipping daily feels like motion, but if nothing changes for the customer it isn't.

drafts.target_text: If you had to restart today, what would you do differently?
drafts.draft: Sell first, build second. Spent six months making a thing nobody needed to learn that one cold email reveals more than a year of guessing.

drafts.target_text: Tonight I hooked up a BOSS RC-600 Looper to a Strandberg guitar and a Yamaha Genos keyboard. The most surprising thing: I did not gain any wisdom.
drafts.draft: The wisdom is usually that the gear was never the bottleneck. Three chords with rhythm beats six chords without it.

drafts.target_text: 'eBay doesn't need 11,000 employees. It hasn't changed since 2005. I could run it from my house.' Ryan Cohen has my vote.
drafts.draft: What's the actual headcount you'd run eBay with? Cohen says one. Most people would guess 1500. The real answer probably tells us where AI hits first.

drafts.target_text: OpenAI and Anthropic announced almost identical enterprise deployment ventures in the same week. Both racing to lock up PE portfolio companies as distribution.
drafts.draft: PE portfolios are the perfect first wedge. 50 mid-market companies, one buying decision, no procurement nightmare. Whoever signs Vista or Thoma first wins the year.
What's the W? I keep seeing his name and missing the launches. Drop the link.

drafts.target_text: New Anthropic Fellows research: Model Spec Midtraining. Standard alignment methods train on examples of desired behavior, but this can fail to generalize. MSM addresses this.
drafts.draft: Reading the paper now. Does MSM make red-teaming cheaper, or does it shift the failure modes to ones we haven't built tests for yet?

drafts.target_text: Copilot is a case study of how to wreck a brand with AI slop. GitHub Copilot was good. Every other Copilot was rushed and turned the brand into something worse.
drafts.draft: Microsoft did the same thing with Bing. Strong original, then 12 spin-offs that diluted what the name meant. Brand discipline is the hardest part of fast shipping.

drafts.target_text: There are probably only 1,000 truly AI-native companies on earth at 5M ARR or more. AI-native means everything in the business is structured around it from day one.
drafts.draft: What's your sniff test for AI-native? Mine: if you removed the AI tomorrow, would the business still ship the same product or just slower? Most fail that.

drafts.target_text: IT WORKS. Lots of tweaking with AI but soldiers are starting to move around naturally now. First load is heavy because it rigs 128 players.
drafts.draft: Pre-rig server-side and cache by character hash? Or is the rig deterministic enough you can ship the rigged GLB and skip the recompute on second load?

drafts.target_text: AI-run business experiments are interesting up to the point where they waste time of humans who haven't opted in. They need to keep their own operators in the loop.
drafts.draft: The cleanest fix is the agent paying for the human's time at market rate. Once the experiment costs real money it self-selects which interactions are worth running.
